content.problemStatement:
A **peak element** in an array is an element that is **strictly greater** than its adjacent elements.

You are provided with a **0-based index integer array** numsnumsnums of size NNN. Your task is to identify the index of **any one peak element** in the array and return it. If multiple peak elements exist, returning the index of any one of them is acceptable.

For simplicity, assume that the elements just outside the boundaries of the array are considered to be negative infinity. That is:


arr[−1]=−∞arr[-1] = -∞arr[−1]=−∞
arr[arr.length]=−∞arr[arr.length] = -∞arr[arr.length]=−∞
Your solution **must have a time complexity of O(log N).**

Function DeclarationFunction NamefindPeakElementfindPeakElementfindPeakElement – This function finds the index of a peak element in the array.

Parameters
numsnumsnums : A 0-based indexed array of integers where adjacent elements are not equal.
Return Value
Returns a single integer — the **index of any peak element**.
Constraints
1≤T≤10001 \leq T \leq 10001≤T≤1000
1≤N≤10001 \leq N \leq 10001≤N≤1000
−231≤nums[i]≤231−1−2^\text{31} \leq nums[i] \leq 2^\text{31} − 1−231≤nums[i]≤231−1
nums[i]≠nums[i+1]nums[i] ≠ nums[i + 1]nums[i]=nums[i+1] for all valid indices iii
Time complexity must be **O(log n)**

Input Format

The first line contains the number of test cases TTT 

Each test case contains:

The first line contains a single integer NNN — the size of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers representing the array elements.




Output Format

Print a single integer — the index of a peak element.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
In the first test case element 10 at index 1 is a peak because 10 > 5 and 10 > 7.
In the second test case element 6 at index 3 is a peak as 6 > 3 and 6 > 1.

solution.approach:
Initialize two pointers:



left = 0
right = n - 1



Perform the binary search loop:




Compute mid = (left + right) // 2.




Compare arr[mid] and arr[mid + 1]:



If arr[mid] > arr[mid + 1]:
The peak must be in the left part (including mid), so set right = mid.
Else:
The peak is in the right part, so set left = mid + 1.





When the loop ends:



left and right will converge to the same index, which is a valid peak element.
solution.timeComplexity: O(log n) — Each iteration halves the search space.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — Constant extra space (only a few variables).
